summary: Gruber highlights the public launch of Lobe, a visual tool for building custom deep learning models without code, created by designer Mike Matas and co-founders. He draws an analogy between Lobe's relationship to CoreML and Illustrator's relationship to PostScript — making powerful but inaccessible technology usable by non-experts through intuitive visual design. Gruber emphasizes that Lobe exports to both CoreML and TensorFlow, and enthusiastically recommends the introductory tour video to anyone regardless of their existing interest in machine learning.
key_points: Lobe is a no-code visual tool for building, training, and exporting custom deep learning models, created by designer Mike Matas
The tool exports to CoreML (Apple) and TensorFlow, in addition to offering a cloud API for production use
Gruber draws an Illustrator-to-PostScript analogy: Lobe makes CoreML accessible to non-experts the way Illustrator made PostScript accessible
Gruber's enthusiasm is driven largely by Matas's design pedigree and the tool's visual-first approach to a deeply technical domain
money_quotes: Writing CoreML by hand is like writing PostScript by hand — possible, but only by a small number of talented experts.
Lobe is to CoreML what Illustrator was to PostScript — a profoundly powerful tool that exposes the underlying technology to non-experts through an intuitive visual interface.
If you have any interest whatsoever in machine learning, drop what you're doing right now and watch their 13-minute introductory tour.
If you're not interested in machine learning, watch the video anyway and you'll become interested in machine learning.
key_insight: The most impactful developer tools don't simplify the underlying technology — they expose it through intuitive visual interfaces that make experts of non-experts, as Lobe does for deep learning.
